name: Horse Mt (Chromite)
short_description: Collect chromite specimens from this remote BLM location in the Humboldt County serpentine belt. Personal use rockhounding on public land with no permit required.
description:
Horse Mountain sits within the Humboldt County ophiolite complex, a section of ancient oceanic crust thrust onto the North American continent during Mesozoic plate tectonics. The surrounding serpentine rocks host chromite deposits that formed from high-temperature crystallization in the Earth's mantle, now exposed at the surface through uplift and erosion. This BLM location offers rockhounders a chance to collect chromite specimens in their natural geological context, surrounded by the distinctive green serpentine parent rock.

The site sits at moderate elevation on a hillside with serpentine outcrops scattered throughout the area. Chromite appears as small to medium-sized metallic dark gray or black crystals and masses within the weathered serpentine. The terrain is steep and brushy, requiring sturdy hiking boots and careful footing. Water is limited, so bring plenty to drink.

This is a destination for rockhounders with some experience recognizing chromite in the field. The remote location and rough terrain mean you should plan a full day trip and come prepared for variable weather. The collecting is best on exposed weathered outcrops where chromite stands out against the lighter serpentine matrix. Personal use only - keep your finds to 25 lbs per day per BLM regulations.
history: The chromite deposits at Horse Mountain are part of the larger Smartville Block and other ophiolite sequences that were obducted onto the California margin during the Jurassic and Cretaceous. These ultramafic rocks represent sections of ancient oceanic plate that crystallized at depth, with chromite forming in the mantle environment before being brought to the surface by tectonic forces over millions of years.
tips: Search the slopes where serpentine bedrock is exposed and weathered - chromite concentrates along fractures and in the matrix of the host rock. Look for the distinctive metallic luster and dark color of chromite crystals; they are much heavier than the surrounding serpentine and will catch your eye on freshly exposed outcrops. Bring a hand lens to distinguish chromite from magnetite and other dark minerals. A geology hammer or pry bar helps expose fresh rock surfaces where chromite specimens are easier to spot.
terrain_notes: Elevation 4798 ft on moderate to steep slopes with scattered serpentine outcrops. Sparse brush and oak woodland vegetation. Uneven rocky terrain with loose material. Approximately 1 to 2 miles of hiking from parking area over rough foothill topography. Sturdy hiking boots required.
written_directions: From Fortuna, take Highway 36 east toward the inland foothills for approximately 18 miles. Turn north on Dyerville Loop Road and continue for 8 miles into the remote serpentine country. Follow Forest Service roads into the Horse Mountain area (roads become rougher as you approach). Final approach may require high-clearance vehicle. Park at the nearest pullout and hike to exposed serpentine outcrops on the upper slopes.
road_conditions: Forest Service roads become rough and rutted in the final miles. High-clearance vehicle recommended. Road conditions vary seasonally with winter runoff.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Forest Service roads. No developed parking area. Limited capacity - expect to park on road shoulder or small dirt pullouts.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collect specimens only - do not remove large quantities of host rock.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Collect specimens - no bulk removal of host rock
nearest_services: Fortuna, California - approximately 25 miles west. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water.
accessibility_notes: Remote backcountry terrain with significant hiking over steep, rough slopes. Rocky footing and loose material throughout. Exposed outcrops can have steep drop-offs. Not suitable for limited mobility or children. Sturdy hiking boots and experience with rugged terrain essential.

faq.question: What exactly is chromite and how do I identify it at Horse Mountain?
faq.answer: Chromite is an iron chromium oxide mineral (FeCr2O4) that appears as metallic dark gray to black crystals and masses. At Horse Mountain, look for chromite in the serpentine host rock - it has a distinctly heavier weight and metallic luster compared to the lighter green serpentine. Use a hand lens to see the crystal structure, and remember that chromite is non-magnetic, which helps distinguish it from similar-looking magnetite.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect chromite at Horse Mountain in California?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on this BLM land. You can collect up to 25 pounds per day per federal regulations. However, commercial collection and motorized excavation are prohibited. Always practice leave-no-trace ethics and respect the natural landscape.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound for chromite near Fortuna?
faq.answer: May through October offers the best conditions at Horse Mountain. Winter brings heavy rainfall that makes Forest Service roads impassable or dangerous, and visibility is poor. Late spring through early fall provides dry conditions, better road access, and clearer skies for hiking steep terrain safely.

faq.question: How far is the hike from parking to chromite collecting areas at Horse Mountain?
faq.answer: Plan for 1 to 2 miles of hiking from where you park along Forest Service roads. The terrain is steep and rocky with loose material underfoot, so allow 45 minutes to 2 hours depending on your fitness level and which outcrops you explore. The effort is worthwhile - weathered serpentine slopes offer the best chromite specimens.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding chromite at this remote California location?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer or small pry bar to expose fresh rock surfaces, a hand lens for identification, sturdy hiking boots with ankle support, plenty of water (2-3 liters minimum), sunscreen, and a small backpack. Since the area is remote with limited services 25 miles away, also pack a first aid kit, headlamp, and tell someone your planned route. A GPS or map is essential given the rough terrain and limited cell service.

faq.question: Is Horse Mountain a good location for beginner rockhounders, or should I have experience first?
faq.answer: Horse Mountain is best suited for experienced rockhounders with rugged backcountry hiking skills. The remote location, steep terrain, loose rock, and need to recognize chromite in field conditions require knowledge and physical fitness. Beginners should start at easier California rockhounding sites with developed access before attempting this location.

meta_title: Horse Mt Chromite Rockhounding - California BLM
meta_description: Collect chromite specimens at Horse Mountain on California BLM land near Fortuna. No permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Remote serpentine terrain.
